summary: Paul offers practical guidance on marriage, singleness, separation, and faithful service within each believer’s present calling.

outline.title: Mutual Faithfulness in Marriage
outline.summary: Paul calls husbands and wives to mutual care, self-giving intimacy, prayer, and faithfulness according to God’s gifts.

outline.title: Singleness, Marriage, and Separation
outline.summary: Paul addresses unmarried believers, widows, married couples, and households divided by faith with pastoral realism.

outline.title: Faithful in Your Calling
outline.summary: Believers can obey God within varied social circumstances because belonging to Christ is their decisive identity.

outline.title: Undivided Devotion in Troubled Times
outline.summary: In light of present pressures, Paul explains how singleness may allow less distraction in serving the Lord.

outline.title: Freedom to Marry or Remain Single
outline.summary: Marriage and singleness can both honor God when chosen faithfully, wisely, and within devotion to the Lord.

verses.bibleText: Now concerning the things whereof ye wrote unto me: It is good for a man not to touch a woman.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Now concerning the things whereof ye wrote” begins Paul’s response to the Corinthians’ own questions. The sentence “It is good for a man not to touch a woman” probably repeats their wording, although Paul can affirm the goodness of singleness in appropriate circumstances. “Touch” is a familiar euphemism for sexual relations; the subject in verses 1–7 is abstinence within marriage, not a declaration that women or bodies are unclean.

The chapter is deliberative rather than systematic: Paul works through concrete situations and repeatedly distinguishes command, permission, preference, and circumstance. Reading every sentence as the same kind of universal rule produces contradictions Paul himself avoids.

The slogan may have appealed to believers reacting against the sexual disorder of chapters 5–6. Their instinct to pursue holiness was not enough; it needed biblical form. Rejecting immorality by rejecting marital intimacy would replace indulgence with a different misuse of the body. Paul answers both with covenant faithfulness.

Some Corinthians appear to have drawn an ascetic conclusion from the gospel: spiritual people should renounce marital sex. Paul immediately qualifies that conclusion with mutual obligations. He can value celibacy without treating it as a universal command or a holier substance.

The transition from “things whereof ye wrote” appears again at 7:25, 8:1, 12:1, and 16:1. Paul is working through a letter, though we hear only his answers. Reconstruction should therefore remain modest. Calling the opening line a Corinthian slogan is probable because of Paul’s immediate correction and similar slogans in 6:12–13, not because quotation marks survive in the manuscripts.

Roman marriage commonly involved family arrangements, property, status, and an unequal balance of power. Against that setting, Paul will address husband and wife in striking symmetry. The gospel neither dissolves marriage nor leaves its ordinary hierarchy untouched; it calls both spouses to covenant faithfulness under Christ.

wordNotes.explanation: “Touch” functions here as a discreet expression for sexual relations. The phrase likely reflects the Corinthians’ question about abstinence; Paul answers by honoring singleness while rejecting the idea that marital intimacy is spiritually defiling.

verses.bibleText: Nevertheless, to avoid fornication, let every man have his own wife, and let every woman have her own husband.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Because sexual immorality is a real pressure, each man is to have “his own wife” and each woman “her own husband.” The reciprocity is important. Paul does not grant men sexual latitude while demanding fidelity from women. Marriage is exclusive in both directions.

He is not claiming that marriage exists only as a remedy for desire. Scripture gives it purposes of companionship, covenant love, mutual help, and, where God grants, family. Paul addresses the immediate Corinthian question rather than writing a complete theology of marriage.

The phrase “to avoid fornication” is literally “because of sexual immoralities,” using a plural that can point to the pressures and instances present in Corinth. Paul proposes no double standard: each spouse belongs in an exclusive covenant.

“Have” can be ordinary marriage language, not a command for immediate weddings across the congregation. Existing marriages must be honored, and those who cannot live celibately should seek marriage faithfully. Those without the gift should not be shamed into promises they cannot truthfully keep.

Nor does the verse command every person to marry. Verses 7–8 honor singleness as a gift. Its narrower point is that married believers should not use spiritual aspiration to abandon the sexual covenant, and unmarried believers should seek a faithful marriage rather than satisfy desire through immorality.

wordNotes.term: His own wife … her own husband
wordNotes.explanation: The mirrored wording stresses exclusive and reciprocal marriage. Paul grants neither sex a double standard: husband and wife belong to one covenant, and sexual desire is to be expressed within that faithful union.

verses.bibleText: Let the husband render unto the wife due benevolence: and likewise also the wife unto the husband.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Due benevolence” refers to the affection and marital intimacy spouses owe one another. Paul places the obligation on husband and wife alike, reversing the assumption that only the wife exists to meet the husband’s desire.

What is “due” cannot be separated from love, consent, health, and care. The verse gives no spouse a right to coerce sex, disregard pain, or treat the other body as property for use. A forced act contradicts the mutuality Paul is establishing and the self-giving character of Christian marriage.

The older expression “due benevolence” can sound like financial payment, but its setting is conjugal responsibility. The language of debt emphasizes that marriage creates obligations beyond the mood of the moment.

Paul does not describe frequency or technique, leaving couples to wise and caring agreement. A healthy application asks whether both partners are heard, whether affection is mutual, and whether intimacy serves their one-flesh covenant. Pastoral counsel should never intrude for curiosity or dictate details Scripture leaves to the couple.

Marital faithfulness includes learning to speak honestly about desire, limitation, trauma, illness, and changing seasons. The command aims at a shared life in which neither spouse weaponizes deprivation and neither claims entitlement without regard for the other.

wordNotes.explanation: The KJV phrase names the affection and marital intimacy owed within marriage. “Due” marks a covenant responsibility, while “benevolence” keeps that responsibility personal and loving; Paul immediately gives the same obligation to both spouses.

verses.bibleText: The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband: and likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The mutual authority described here was unusually strong in a culture where husbands normally exercised greater legal and social power. A wife has authority over her husband’s body in the same sentence that he has authority over hers. Neither stands outside the covenant’s claims.

The verbs are exactly parallel: the wife does not exercise independent authority over her body, and neither does the husband. That symmetry was not the ordinary Roman legal arrangement. The gospel places the socially stronger spouse under the same embodied obligation as the weaker.

Mutual authority is best understood as mutual self-gift, not exchanged ownership. The body still belongs to God, as 6:19–20 has established. A spouse receives no authority to command what God forbids, to expose the other to disease, or to ignore the other’s personhood.

The verse’s reciprocity anticipates 11:11–12, where man and woman are mutually dependent in the Lord. Paul can speak within ancient household structures while planting a theological principle that judges domination. Any application that quotes the wife’s lack of independent authority and omits the husband’s identical condition has altered the apostolic sentence.

This mutual belonging corrects both selfish independence and possession. Spouses do not say, “My body is mine, so your need never matters,” or, “Your body is mine, so your will never matters.” They receive one another as persons who first belong to the Lord.

wordNotes.term: Hath not power of … own body
wordNotes.explanation: “Power” here means authority. Paul’s exact parallel places husband and wife under mutual bodily obligations, not unilateral ownership. The verse calls each spouse to self-giving faithfulness and cannot rightly be used to excuse coercion or abuse.

verses.bibleText: Defraud ye not one the other, except it be with consent for a time, that ye may give yourselves to fasting and prayer; and come together again, that Satan tempt you not for your incontinency.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Defraud ye not one the other” speaks of depriving a spouse of marital intimacy. Paul permits abstinence only by mutual consent, for a limited season, and for concentrated prayer, followed by reunion. Every qualification blocks ascetic pressure imposed by one partner.

The reference to fasting appears in later manuscripts and the KJV; the central emphasis in the earliest textual tradition is prayer. Either way, temporary abstinence serves a specific spiritual purpose and does not make sexual relations spiritually dirty.

Prayer can at times call for focused abstinence, as other demanding duties may limit ordinary marital rhythms. Paul refuses an open-ended spiritual rationale. Consent, a defined season, and reunion protect the marriage from one person’s unilateral vow.

The verse recognizes differing capacities for self-control without assigning blame to the spouse with stronger desire. When trauma, pain, medication, disability, or illness affects intimacy, wise medical and pastoral support may be needed. Satan’s warning cannot justify pressure, and temptation excuses neither coercion nor adultery.

The command “come together again” identifies ordinary marital reunion as the proper end of the prayer season. Devotion to God and affection for a spouse are not rivals. A spiritual practice is disordered if it predictably produces pride, resentment, or vulnerability to sin while refusing Paul’s safeguards.

wordNotes.explanation: Temporary abstinence requires shared agreement and a defined season. Even prayer does not authorize one spouse to impose permanent deprivation; Paul joins spiritual devotion with mutual care and directs the couple to come together again.

verses.bibleText: But I speak this by permission, and not of commandment.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“I speak this by permission, and not of commandment” most naturally refers to the permitted period of abstinence just described, though some take it more broadly of Paul’s marital counsel. He allows a temporary exception; he does not command couples to suspend intimacy for prayer.

The demonstrative “this” has an immediate antecedent in the temporary abstinence of verse 5. That reading makes good sense: Paul grants such a practice but does not require it. Others take “this” to encompass the foregoing marital directions; the result still distinguishes pastoral accommodation from a universal command to marry.

Paul’s method is useful beyond marriage. Scripture can commend a discipline without making it obligatory for every believer. Voluntary fasting, singleness, and other practices retain their spiritual value only when they remain governed by grace and truthful calling.

The distinction illustrates Paul’s careful pastoral speech. Some matters carry a direct command of the Lord; others require apostolic judgment adapted to circumstance. Both can be authoritative without pretending every piece of advice has identical form. Concession also protects conscience. A devotional practice becomes spiritually dangerous when a voluntary discipline is turned into a universal test of holiness.

wordNotes.term: By permission, and not of commandment
wordNotes.explanation: Paul distinguishes a pastoral concession from a universal command. The statement concerns the arrangement he has just described, showing that apostolic counsel can address circumstances without turning every permitted practice into an obligation for all believers.

verses.bibleText: For I would that all men were even as I myself. But every man hath his proper gift of God, one after this manner, and another after that.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul can wish that others shared his unmarried freedom, yet he refuses to make his calling the norm. “Every man hath his proper gift of God, one after this manner, and another after that.” Marriage and singleness can both be charismata—gifts of grace suited to different lives and services.

The verse does not imply that every present circumstance feels effortless. Gifts are often lived through discipline, community support, and growth. Still, a supposed calling that requires perpetual deceit or destroys another person should be reexamined.

Paul’s precise marital history is unknown. Suggestions that he was widowed, divorced, or always single go beyond the evidence. “Even as I myself” safely establishes only his present unmarried state and capacity.

Charisma does not mean that discernment is effortless or that a person receives an unmistakable label. Desire, opportunity, responsibilities, and capacity for faithful service all inform the decision. The church helps by providing examples and community rather than treating a quick marriage as the default solution to every uncertainty.

Gift language also discourages envy. The married person should not idealize a single person’s flexibility while ignoring loneliness and unsupported burdens; the single person should not idealize companionship while ignoring covenant demands. Grace is learned from inside an actual calling, with honest recognition of both good and cost.

Christian communities honor this diversity when they stop treating marriage as completion of an incomplete adult and stop treating celibacy as a superior spiritual class. Fulness is found in Christ; each state becomes a field for faithful love.

wordNotes.explanation: A grace-gift given by God. In this context both celibate singleness and married life can be received as distinct callings rather than ranked as spiritual classes.

verses.bibleText: I say therefore to the unmarried and widows, It is good for them if they abide even as I.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul addresses “the unmarried and widows.” The first term may include widowers or others no longer married, rather than every never-married person, since “virgins” receive separate attention in verse 25. Exact boundaries are debated.

Remaining as Paul is can be “good.” In Roman society, remarriage was often expected and at times legally encouraged, particularly for women of childbearing age. Paul opens a socially unusual space in which a widow can live an honored, purposeful life without a husband.

Widows in the ancient world could face economic vulnerability and pressure from extended family. Others, especially with resources or household connections, possessed meaningful independence. Paul’s permission to remain single should be joined to the church’s duty to honor and support those without a spouse.

His counsel also prevents bereavement from becoming a deadline. A widow or widower does not owe society a replacement marriage, and continued love for a deceased spouse does not make a new marriage wrong. Verse 39 leaves the future genuinely open under the Lord.

This is counsel, not a prohibition. Singleness is good when it can be received truthfully and supported by Christian community. Churches should not praise the state abstractly while leaving single adults isolated or excluded from meaningful service.

wordNotes.explanation: “Abide” means remain in the unmarried state Paul himself occupies. He commends this course as genuinely good, not compulsory; the surrounding verses treat singleness and marriage as differing gifts rather than higher and lower spiritual ranks.

verses.bibleText: But if they cannot contain, let them marry: for it is better to marry than to burn.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If unmarried believers “cannot contain”—that is, do not exercise the self-control needed for celibacy—Paul says they should marry. “Better to marry than to burn” most naturally refers to being consumed with sexual desire, though the final judgment may echo at the edges of the warning.

Marriage is not a license to transfer uncontrolled desire onto a spouse. It requires consent, fidelity, patience, and responsibility. Paul’s point is that honest covenant is better than a spiritual pose that repeatedly leads toward immorality.

“Burn” is vivid but not a diagnosis that any sexual desire proves absence of the gift of singleness. Desire can be governed; Paul has just spoken of self-control. The issue is a continuing condition in which the person is unable to sustain celibate faithfulness.

Marriage should not be presented as treatment for compulsive sexual behavior. Such behavior can persist and wound a spouse. Repentance, accountability, and appropriate therapy may be necessary before or within marriage. Covenant provides a holy home for sexuality; it does not automatically create mature love.

Neither desire nor anxiety should produce a hurried and unwise marriage. The verse recognizes embodied reality without making desire the only criterion. Character, shared faith, safety, and willingness to keep covenant remain essential.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase means lacking sustained self-control in this matter, not lacking human worth. Paul advises marriage rather than a celibate promise that cannot be faithfully kept. Marriage is honorable, though it is never a license to use another person.

verses.bibleText: And unto the married I command, yet not I, but the Lord, Let not the wife depart from her husband:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
When Paul speaks to married believers, he invokes a command of “the Lord”: the wife is not to separate from her husband. Jesus taught the permanence of marriage and opposed the casual use of divorce. Paul distinguishes a remembered saying of Jesus from situations on which he must give fresh apostolic application in verse 12.

Marriage is a covenant intended for lifelong fidelity, not an arrangement discarded whenever affection cools. In a Roman setting where divorce could be initiated with little formal process, this command placed both husband and wife under Christ’s claim.

The command corresponds with Jesus’ teaching that humans must not separate what God joined. Paul addresses wife first, perhaps because women in the Corinthian correspondence were particularly considering separation; the text does not give enough detail to know.

“Depart” can describe separation or divorce within a Roman setting where formal legal procedure was minimal. Modern legal categories do not map neatly onto every ancient term, so the moral center—do not repudiate covenant casually—should guide interpretation.

Safety planning is not covenant contempt. A person who leaves immediate danger may still desire repentance, justice, and whatever restoration is morally possible. The phrase “not I, but the Lord” demonstrates Paul’s respect for the historical teaching of Jesus. Apostolic inspiration does not erase sources or flatten them into a voice without history. Paul remembers, transmits, and applies a command spoken in Jesus’ earthly ministry.

The verse must not be used to trap someone in violence, sexual abuse, or credible danger. Separation for protection can be morally necessary; verse 11 itself recognizes that separation may occur. Upholding covenant includes opposing the conduct that destroys its safety and truth.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul appeals to teaching already given by Jesus concerning the permanence of marriage. The contrast does not make Paul’s later counsel uninspired; it distinguishes a saying of the Lord the church already knew from apostolic guidance for a situation Jesus had not directly addressed.

verses.bibleText: But and if she depart, let her remain unmarried or be reconciled to her husband: and let not the husband put away his wife.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If separation has occurred, Paul names two paths: remain unmarried or be reconciled, and the husband is likewise not to divorce his wife. Reconciliation is the desired restoration of a covenant, not a demand for immediate return without evidence of repentance and safety.

“Remain unmarried” shows that Paul does not treat the separation as if nothing happened. A new union is not the immediate solution to marital pain. Time may be needed for truth, legal protection, repentance, and discernment.

Yet reconciliation is not a single emotional moment. Where serious harm occurred, it may require demonstrated change over a sustained period, accountability to competent people, and the free consent of the harmed spouse. Forgiveness can be offered without instant restoration of access.

The equal command to the husband opposes a culture in which male initiative in divorce could be treated more lightly. The instruction addresses voluntary marital rupture in the situation before Paul. It does not supply a complete case law for every form of abuse, abandonment, adultery, legal protection, or remarriage. Jesus’ teaching names sexual unfaithfulness as a grave covenant breach; verse 15 will discuss desertion by an unbelieving spouse.

The command to remain unmarried or reconcile also protects the abandoned partner from immediate replacement while a covenant may still be repairable. It should never be used to keep a person in indefinite limbo through an offender’s occasional promises. Churches need clear processes for determining whether repentance is credible and whether desertion has become settled.

Where restoration is considered, truth must precede appearance. Accountability, restitution, treatment, and sustained change may be necessary. A church should neither make divorce casual nor make continued danger the price of proving faithfulness.

wordNotes.explanation: Reconciliation is the restoration Paul names when separated spouses can safely and faithfully repair the marriage. The command does not require someone to remain exposed to violence; genuine reconciliation includes repentance, truth, and conduct consistent with covenant peace.

verses.bibleText: But to the rest speak I, not the Lord: If any brother hath a wife that believeth not, and she be pleased to dwell with him, let him not put her away.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“To the rest speak I, not the Lord” does not mean Paul’s counsel is uninspired or optional. He means that Jesus’ recorded teaching did not directly address a marriage in which one spouse becomes a Christian and the other remains a pagan. Paul now applies the gospel with apostolic authority.

If the unbelieving wife is willing to live with her believing husband, he must not divorce her. Conversion does not erase a valid marriage or make the other spouse contaminating.

Paul’s “I” remains the voice of an apostle commissioned by Christ. Verse 25 uses similar language of trustworthy judgment, and verse 40 claims the Spirit’s guidance. The contrast is between a direct remembered dominical saying and a new case, not between divine and merely human portions of Scripture.

Mixed marriages likely arose when one spouse converted after the wedding. This differs from a believer deliberately entering a marriage with someone who does not share faith, which verse 39’s “only in the Lord” addresses.

The believer’s continued presence can become a long obedience of love, never an excuse to compromise worship or use new faith as a pretext for abandonment. Paul assumes willingness to dwell is shown in real shared life, not merely a label; coercive control or sabotage of worship contradicts it. Respectful accommodation may preserve peace in food, schedule, and family custom, but worship belongs to God and conscience cannot be handed to a spouse.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul now addresses a mixed-faith marriage for which no recorded saying of Jesus directly supplied the answer. He is not withdrawing divine authority from his counsel; he is carefully identifying its apostolic form and its new missionary setting.

verses.bibleText: And the woman which hath an husband that believeth not, and if he be pleased to dwell with her, let her not leave him.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul gives the same command directly to a believing woman with an unbelieving husband. The symmetry recognizes her as a moral agent responsible before the Lord, not merely as a dependent whose religion is determined by her husband.

Roman women could initiate divorce in Paul’s period, although social and economic consequences varied sharply by class. The gospel does not encourage a Christian wife to use that possibility simply because religious difference is difficult.

Addressing the wife directly gives her responsibility to discern whether the husband is willing to “dwell” with her. Consent to shared life involves more than occupying the same building; it entails continuing the marriage rather than punishing conversion through rejection.

Economic dependence could make a pagan husband’s departure devastating. Paul’s call to peace places responsibility on the congregation to become family and material support, not merely to quote release to the abandoned woman.

Religious difference alone is not abuse, and patient fidelity remains the counsel where willing shared life continues. Yet her decision to remain never requires idolatry or unsafe cohabitation; dwelling together includes respect for her worship and person. If the husband repudiates that shared life, the situation moves toward the departure addressed in verse 15.

wordNotes.explanation: The unbelieving husband’s willingness to continue the shared household is decisive. Conversion does not itself dissolve a marriage. Where peaceful life remains possible, the believing wife is not told to abandon the covenant merely because her spouse does not share her faith.

verses.bibleText: For the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified by the husband: else were your children unclean; but now are they holy.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The unbelieving spouse is “sanctified” through the believer, and their children are “holy.” Paul cannot mean automatic conversion or moral purity; verse 16 still asks whether the spouse will be saved. Holiness here describes a household set within the sphere of covenant blessing and gospel witness rather than rendered unclean by religious difference.

The concern may answer believers who feared that marital sex with a pagan spouse defiled them or that their children were illegitimate before God. Paul says the opposite: the believer’s presence brings consecrating influence into the home.

“Sanctified” is perfect tense, describing a settled household relation resulting from the believing spouse’s presence. It cannot mean that the unbeliever’s choices are overridden; holiness here is relational consecration, not inward regeneration.

Children are “holy” rather than “unclean,” which likely answers a fear about the legitimacy or religious status of the household. Paul reassures rather than supplying a ritual rule.

The verse encourages active family witness: age-appropriate biblical teaching, prayer, consistent character, and freedom from contempt toward the unbelieving parent. Children should not be recruited into marital conflict or made responsible to convert a parent.

Household holiness is hopeful without being possessive. God’s gracious claim creates opportunity, not ownership of another’s response. A believing parent can trust God’s nearness to the family while recognizing children as persons who must one day choose faith rather than extensions of the parent’s religious standing.

This verse does not by itself establish infant baptism or guarantee a child’s salvation. Each person must respond to God. It does assure a believing parent that the family may be embraced as a field of prayer, love, instruction, and hope.

wordNotes.explanation: These family members are “holy” in the sense of being set within the gracious influence and covenant privileges of a Christian household. The words do not promise automatic salvation; each person still responds to God, but the marriage and children are not religiously defiled.

verses.bibleText: But if the unbelieving depart, let him depart. A brother or a sister is not under bondage in such cases: but God hath called us to peace.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If the unbelieving spouse chooses to leave, “let him depart.” The brother or sister is “not under bondage” in such cases, because God has called believers to peace. Paul does not require desperate control of someone who has repudiated the shared life.

At minimum, the believer is free from guilt for the other person’s departure and from an endless obligation to make cohabitation happen. Many interpreters also understand settled desertion as releasing the marital bond. Paul does not discuss every condition for remarriage here, so counsel should consider the whole biblical witness, the finality of the abandonment, possibilities of reconciliation, and the person’s safety.

The perfect passive “not enslaved” describes an enduring release from bondage in the stated circumstance. Debate continues over whether it technically dissolves the marriage. Paul’s language is stronger than “not at fault,” but he does not explicitly say “free to marry” as he does of a widow in verse 39.

That uncertainty calls for humility in difficult cases. A simple rule can overlook fraudulent desertion, repeated return and departure, abuse that functions as constructive abandonment, or genuine prospects of repair.

What is certain is that God’s call to peace opposes coercion. No church should threaten an abandoned believer with spiritual condemnation for failing to control another adult’s decision.

The believer is called “brother or sister,” placing male and female desertion under the same release. The church must not offer compassion to an abandoned husband while treating an abandoned wife as responsible to preserve the marriage through greater submission. Paul’s symmetry protects both consciences.

Peace is not the appearance of marriage maintained through fear, threats, or coercion. It is life ordered by truth and righteousness. The verse comforts the deserted without turning every temporary separation into automatic permission for a new union.

wordNotes.term: Not under bondage — οὐ δεδούλωται (ou dedoulōtai)
wordNotes.explanation: Not enslaved or held in bondage. At minimum, a deserted believer is not morally responsible to compel an unbelieving spouse to remain; Paul joins this release to God’s call to peace.

verses.bibleText: For what knowest thou, O wife, whether thou shalt save thy husband? or how knowest thou, O man, whether thou shalt save thy wife?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The questions to wife and husband can be read hopefully—perhaps God will use faithful witness to save the spouse—or soberly—no believer can know or guarantee that outcome. Both notes suit the context.

The KJV’s “how knowest thou ... whether thou shalt save” can sound as though the believer saves. Biblical usage often speaks of a human instrument “saving” others while God remains the effective Savior. The spouse’s witness can be a means; grace and response belong to God and the unbeliever.

The question’s uncertainty also protects consent. Evangelistic hope does not permit a believing spouse to monitor, badger, or manipulate. A quiet, credible life may speak more powerfully than constant argument.

Where separation has occurred, prayer can continue without pursuit that violates boundaries. Trusting God means acknowledging that his reach exceeds the believer’s control. A spouse may pray, speak, repent, and embody the gospel, but cannot produce conversion. Christ alone saves. This releases the believer from manipulation and from the crushing belief that another person’s destiny depends entirely on preserving the marriage at any cost.

The uncertainty of the question can also restrain someone considering an unequally yoked marriage on the assumption that the partner will convert later. Paul is counseling people already in mixed marriages, not recommending that believers create them as evangelistic strategy. “Only in the Lord” remains the guidance for a free choice of spouse.

The unbelieving spouse should not be reduced to a project. Genuine love honors present personhood while desiring eternal good. Faithful presence remains appropriate when the spouse consents to the marriage; peaceful release becomes necessary when the spouse decisively refuses it.

wordNotes.explanation: The believing spouse may become an instrument through whom the other hears and sees the gospel, but only God saves and the outcome is not guaranteed. Paul therefore offers hope without making one spouse responsible for controlling the other’s response.

verses.bibleText: But as God hath distributed to every man, as the Lord hath called every one, so let him walk. And so ordain I in all churches.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul broadens the principle: each person should walk in the situation the Lord has “distributed” and in which God called him or her. Conversion establishes a new allegiance without requiring believers to abandon every existing marriage, occupation, ethnic identity, or social location.

“So ordain I in all churches” shows that this is not a private compromise for Corinth. The gospel creates stability as well as change. People need not manufacture dramatic external disruption to prove that conversion is real.

“As God hath distributed” recognizes providence in differing circumstances, yet providence must not be confused with moral approval of everything that exists. God can call a slave under an unjust system without calling the system good.

“Let him walk” makes the principle active. The believer brings discipleship into the present setting: marriage becomes service, work becomes honest vocation, cultural identity becomes an arena for witness.

Paul’s translocal rule also resists spiritual tourism—the idea that another congregation, culture, or life state is always where real faithfulness will begin. The principle is not an absolute command to remain in abuse, slavery, illegal work, or any condition that itself requires sin. Paul allows departure in verse 15 and freedom in verse 21. Calling transforms how circumstances are inhabited and supplies wisdom about when faithfulness requires change.

wordNotes.explanation: God’s allotment, Christ’s call, and the believer’s daily conduct belong together. Conversion gives a new allegiance that can be lived within varied circumstances; “walk” describes an ongoing manner of life, not passive acceptance of every injustice.

verses.bibleText: Is any man called being circumcised? let him not become uncircumcised. Is any called in uncircumcision? let him not be circumcised.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
A circumcised man should not seek to remove the physical signs of circumcision; an uncircumcised man should not submit to circumcision as though it were required for standing in Christ. Some Jewish men in the Greek world attempted surgical concealment to avoid stigma in baths or athletics, while Gentile converts could face pressure to adopt Jewish identity.

Paul tells both groups to remain without shame or boasting. The gospel does not require Jewish believers to erase their history, nor Gentile believers to become ethnic Jews.

Attempts to conceal circumcision are documented in Greek athletic culture, where male nudity made the mark visible and sometimes shameful. Paul refuses shame-driven reversal. Jewish believers may remain visibly Jewish without using that identity to rank Gentiles.

Likewise, Gentile Christians do not need circumcision to complete baptism or gain access to God. The Jerusalem council and Galatians address the controversy more fully.

Christian unity is not cultural flattening. It permits histories to remain while removing their power to determine acceptance and honor before God. The verse addresses covenant status, not medical correction or cultural dress. Its force lies in refusing bodily alteration as a means of achieving spiritual rank.

wordNotes.explanation: Some ancient men attempted to conceal circumcision, but Paul’s larger point is theological: neither erasing Jewish identity nor adopting its physical marker secures standing in Christ. Believers need not refashion their bodies to gain spiritual status.

verses.bibleText: Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.” Paul distinguishes an ethnic covenant marker from the obedient life to which grace calls all believers.

The statement cannot mean that God’s moral law has been abolished. Paul explicitly says commandment-keeping matters, and chapters 5–6 have applied commands concerning sexuality, idolatry, honesty, and love. Obedience does not earn justification; it is the response of people already called in Christ and empowered by the Spirit.

Paul’s distinction assumes that the law contains enduring moral claims and covenant signs whose administration changes across salvation history. Circumcision pointed to covenant membership; in Christ, Jew and Gentile enter the one people through faith and receive heart circumcision by the Spirit.

The commandments remain coherent in love of God and neighbor, not as isolated regulations manipulated for merit. The seventh-day Sabbath, grounded in creation and included in God’s moral law, is not addressed directly here but cannot be erased by a verse that explicitly commends obedience.

Grace writes obedience into life; it does not replace obedience with ethnic neutrality. Paul can relativize circumcision while affirming commandments because not every biblical command functions identically after Christ. The apostolic writings themselves show which covenant signs are fulfilled and how moral instruction continues. This requires canonical interpretation, not a simple slogan that either preserves every Israelite ordinance unchanged or dismisses law as a whole.

The verse also prevents commandment-keeping from becoming a badge of ethnic or spiritual superiority. God’s law reaches the heart and common life. Its Sabbath, sexual, economic, and relational claims are honored as gifts from the Creator, not used as currency by which sinners purchase grace.

wordNotes.term: Keeping of the commandments of God
wordNotes.explanation: Ethnic boundary markers cannot replace obedience. Paul does not dismiss God’s moral law; he contrasts circumcision status with the faithful life God commands. Grace creates allegiance expressed in love and obedience rather than earning salvation through ritual identity.

verses.bibleText: Let every man abide in the same calling wherein he was called.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul repeats the call to remain in the condition in which one was called. Repetition gives the section its frame and counters restless attempts to secure spiritual significance through a changed label.

“Calling” can mean both God’s summons and the circumstances in which that summons reached a person. The circumstances do not become sacred simply because God found someone there; they cease to determine worth.

The imperative “abide” is pastoral medicine for a congregation agitated about status. Constant comparison can make any current life appear unusable to God. Paul gives believers permission to stop waiting for a more impressive identity.

At the same time, calling can generate gradual transformation. A merchant may change dishonest practice, a household may learn mutuality, and an enslaved believer may receive freedom. The stable center is belonging to Christ, not immobility in every external detail.

Discernment asks whether change is pursued from obedience and love or from the need to become worthy. Contentment here is active faithfulness, not passivity. A believer can pursue education, lawful employment, freedom, or marriage without imagining that God will become present only after the external change. Service begins now, “with God,” as verse 24 will say.

wordNotes.explanation: “Calling” refers to the circumstances in which God’s summons reached a person, not a divine endorsement of every social condition. Paul counsels steadiness against restless status-seeking while allowing freedom when a real opportunity arises.

verses.bibleText: Art thou called being a servant? care not for it: but if thou mayest be made free, use it rather.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
To the enslaved believer Paul says, “care not for it”—do not let the status define standing before God. Ancient slavery ranged from brutal labor to household and administrative roles, but enslaved people lacked the secure legal personhood of the free and could be bought, sold, or abused. The verse must not be used to soften that injustice.

Roman slavery was not identical in every respect to modern race-based chattel slavery, but difference must not be used to minimize it. Enslaved persons remained subject to ownership and could suffer severe exploitation. Roles ranged from mine labor and domestic service to skilled trade and administration.

Manumission was possible and common enough to be socially visible, though it depended largely on owners and did not erase patronal ties. This setting explains both parts of Paul’s counsel: present status cannot exclude a person from Christ, and available freedom is worth using.

The gospel’s brotherhood ultimately contradicts any system that treats image-bearers as property. The second clause is debated. It may mean “even if you can become free, use your present condition,” or, more likely, “if freedom becomes available, make use of it.” The latter fits Paul’s practical exceptions elsewhere in the chapter and recognizes freedom as a genuine good.

Historical nuance about Roman slavery must not become moral relativism. Educated or administratively powerful slaves were still owned, and opportunities for manumission were not rights equally available to all. Paul’s assurance gives dignity within a condition he cannot immediately remove; Christian history is culpable wherever it converted that pastoral word into theological support for owning humans. Paul addresses the enslaved pastorally; he does not endorse the enslaver. Spiritual dignity is available now, and lawful liberation may rightly be received.

wordNotes.explanation: A debated phrase in 7:21. In context it most likely encourages an enslaved believer to make use of an available opportunity for freedom while refusing to let present status determine worth before Christ.

verses.bibleText: For he that is called in the Lord, being a servant, is the Lord's freeman: likewise also he that is called, being free, is Christ's servant.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The enslaved Christian is “the Lord’s freeman,” while the legally free Christian is “Christ’s servant.” Paul reverses social rank under one lordship. Neither Roman status can define the person’s ultimate position.

This spiritual equality contains social consequences. At the Lord’s table, an enslaved believer is a brother or sister, not a lesser kind of Christian. A free member cannot appeal to civic status to demand greater honor in the body.

Apeleutheros, “freedman,” was also a recognized social category: a formerly enslaved person released but often still connected to a patron. Paul calls the enslaved believer Christ’s freed person before civil release occurs.

The free believer becomes Christ’s doulos, servant or slave. The reversal removes boasting without making the two masteries morally equivalent. Christ gave himself for the servant and commands masters to answer to him.

At the table and in the assembly, these identities require equal honor. A church that seats, feeds, or hears people according to civil rank has denied the freedom it proclaims.

The reversal also speaks to self-understanding. A slave may internalize society’s verdict of inferiority; a free person may imagine autonomy. Christ tells the first, “You are free in me,” and the second, “You are accountable to me.” Grace addresses each status at the point of its spiritual danger.

The metaphor of serving Christ does not make human slavery harmless. Christ’s mastery is liberating, just, and self-giving—the opposite of ownership that treats a person as a thing. All believers, free and enslaved, answer to him.

wordNotes.explanation: The gospel reverses ordinary rankings. An enslaved believer possesses freedom in the Lord, while a socially free believer belongs wholly to Christ. Neither civil status establishes spiritual superiority, and both identities challenge human domination.

verses.bibleText: Ye are bought with a price; be not ye the servants of men.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Ye are bought with a price” repeats 6:20. Christ’s redemption relativizes every competing claim, so Paul commands, “be not ye the servants of men.” The verse may warn free people against selling themselves into slavery for economic security, but it also speaks more broadly against surrendering conscience to human control.

Paul cannot be telling existing slaves simply to stop obeying in a way that would expose them to punishment; verse 21 addressed their actual condition. He gives them a deeper identity from which slavery’s total claim is denied.

Some economically vulnerable free people could sell themselves into slavery or attach themselves strongly to a patron. Paul’s command may warn against such voluntary dependence, especially if it compromises allegiance.

The wider principle applies to debt, employment, political loyalty, and religious leadership: believers may enter legitimate obligations but must not surrender the conscience Christ purchased.

“Price” returns the argument to the cross. Freedom is not the self-created independence prized by Corinth. It is belonging to the Lord who releases people to obey God rather than fear human masters.

Religious slavery can appear when a leader claims revelation no one may test, controls marriage or work choices, or threatens divine rejection for leaving the group. Paul has spent four chapters refusing personal ownership of believers. Being bought by Christ is a reason to resist such domination, not a metaphor leaders may borrow for themselves.

No Christian leader may use spiritual authority to demand the kind of possession Christ has reserved for himself. Redemption creates responsible service and freedom of conscience, not religious bondage.

wordNotes.explanation: Christ’s redemptive claim sets the believer’s ultimate ownership. Because God paid the cost of redemption, no human master, patron, faction, or public opinion may command the conscience in Christ’s place.

verses.bibleText: Brethren, let every man, wherein he is called, therein abide with God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The refrain concludes with “abide with God.” God is present in the believer’s current location, whether married or single, circumcised or uncircumcised, enslaved or free. That phrase keeps remaining from becoming stoic resignation.

External status is neither the source nor the limit of communion with God. A person may seek wise change without treating the present as spiritually empty.

The phrase “therein abide” is qualified by “with God,” which turns location into vocation. God is neither closer to the married than the single nor closer to the socially free than the enslaved.

Community is necessary for this counsel to be credible. Telling someone to serve God in a low-status condition while refusing solidarity can sanctify neglect. Paul’s own churches crossed household lines, gathered rich and poor, and called them family.

Abiding with God includes receiving the body’s help and joining efforts toward just freedom where possible. “With God” also tests the condition. Remaining in work that requires fraud or in a home where another person is in danger cannot be sanctified by passivity. The question is whether the situation can be inhabited in faithful fellowship with God; if not, obedience may require change and help.

wordNotes.explanation: Remaining is never mere resignation, for the decisive phrase is “with God.” Whatever a believer’s lawful station, life is to be consciously lived in God’s presence, under his authority, and open to the faithful changes his providence permits.

verses.bibleText: Now concerning virgins I have no commandment of the Lord: yet I give my judgment, as one that hath obtained mercy of the Lord to be faithful.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Now concerning virgins” introduces another item from the Corinthians’ letter. The term most naturally refers to never-married women, probably in betrothal situations discussed in verses 36–38. Paul has no recorded command from Jesus addressing this exact circumstance, but he offers judgment as one who has received mercy to be trustworthy.

That wording combines modesty and authority. He does not invent a saying of Jesus, nor does he present pastoral judgment as a random opinion.

“No commandment of the Lord” also prevents false certainty. Paul knows where the Jesus tradition directly speaks and where he is reasoning from gospel principles. His honesty strengthens rather than weakens the judgment.

“Obtained mercy ... to be faithful” links trustworthiness to grace. Paul does not claim natural infallibility or superiority; mercy appointed him a steward whose considered counsel deserves attention.

Pastoral application should preserve that tone: difficult questions require clear convictions and candid admission of what the text does not settle. The noun for “virgin” is feminine, but Paul later addresses a man in relation to “his virgin.” That shift leaves several reconstructions possible—a daughter or ward, a fiancée, or a celibate partnership—and none should be presented as certain.

The status of “virgin” carries no automatic spiritual superiority. Paul’s counsel will depend on distress, freedom for service, desire, and settled conviction. Marriage remains good and permitted.

wordNotes.explanation: “Judgment” means Paul’s considered pastoral opinion on a matter for which he cites no direct command from Jesus. He speaks as a recipient of mercy entrusted to be faithful, combining humility about the form of his counsel with confidence in his calling.

verses.bibleText: I suppose therefore that this is good for the present distress, I say, that it is good for a man so to be.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The “present distress” or necessity is not identified. It may involve famine, economic pressure, persecution, the general troubles expected before Christ’s coming, or a combination. Paul’s deliberate lack of detail counsels restraint.

The noun anankē can mean distress, pressure, or necessity. Because Paul calls it “present,” readers should not identify it solely with a future tribulation. Corinth and the wider Mediterranean world could face food shortages, political instability, and local hostility.

The crisis also stands within Paul’s expectation of Christ’s near return. Local and eschatological pressures reinforce each other: the world’s fragility makes new obligations weightier.

Since Paul does not name the event, contemporary application should use the principle rather than pretend to reconstruct certainty. War, persecution, displacement, or severe financial crisis may alter what is prudent without redefining marriage.

Because of that pressure, remaining in one’s present state is “good.” Beginning a marriage during crisis brings additional responsibilities and vulnerabilities. This is prudential counsel, not a declaration that marriage is intrinsically inferior.

Paul’s judgment is “that it is good for a man so to be,” a deliberately broad phrase. He recommends continuity rather than identifying one status as the good. The same crisis can make both maintaining a marriage and remaining unmarried wiser than disruptive change.

Advice shaped by a particular distress should not be universalized without regard to changed conditions. Its enduring wisdom is to consider whether a contemplated commitment can be sustained faithfully in the real circumstances God’s people face.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase points to an acute pressure already affecting or approaching the Corinthians, though its exact nature is uncertain. Paul’s preference for remaining as one is therefore situational counsel shaped by hardship, not a declaration that marriage is inherently inferior.

verses.bibleText: Art thou bound unto a wife? seek not to be loosed. Art thou loosed from a wife? seek not a wife.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The married person should not seek release, and the unmarried person need not urgently seek a spouse. Paul applies his stability principle to both sides without making either state permanent by command.

“Loosed from a wife” may include widowed, divorced, or otherwise unmarried men; the exact group is uncertain. The verse does not provide permission for a man to abandon an existing wife in order to pursue Paul’s preference for singleness.

The two commands are not perfectly parallel. A person already bound in marriage carries covenant obligation; one currently free has no duty to search anxiously. Existing promises have moral weight that unrealized possibilities do not.

This distinction protects Paul’s preference from harming spouses. Spiritual enthusiasm cannot transform a marriage vow into a lower calling that may be discarded. For the unmarried, “seek not” need not forbid prayer, friendship, or wise discernment. It removes desperation and the assumption that life cannot begin until a partner appears.

Neither does it forbid a single person from discerning marriage. Verse 28 explicitly permits it. Paul removes anxious striving rather than closing the future.

wordNotes.explanation: “Bound” describes the marriage bond; “loosed” describes being unmarried or released from it. Under present pressure Paul advises against urgently changing either state, yet the next verse makes clear that choosing marriage is not sin.

verses.bibleText: But and if thou marry, thou hast not sinned; and if a virgin marry, she hath not sinned. Nevertheless such shall have trouble in the flesh: but I spare you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Marriage is “no sin.” Paul says it twice, for a man and for a virgin woman, protecting both from ascetic condemnation. His concern is “trouble in the flesh”—the concrete pressures marriage may bring during the present crisis.

Such trouble can include economic burden, exposure of a spouse to persecution, divided practical responsibilities, and the ordinary sorrows attached to loving another person in a fragile world. Paul wishes to spare them, not frighten them with an impure view of sex.

Paul’s “nevertheless” is important. He has recommended remaining as one is, but he anticipates the scrupulous conscience and says marriage does not sin. The same gospel that calls people beyond convention also frees them from ascetic fear.

“Trouble in the flesh” is not punishment for marrying. Flesh here can simply mean embodied, earthly life rather than sinful nature. A spouse’s needs become one’s own exposure; love increases both joy and vulnerability.

Paul’s desire to “spare” them is fatherly prudence. He shares a cost they may not yet see while leaving the decision free. “I spare you” makes pastoral intent explicit. Paul is not preserving his authority or an institutional ideal; he is considering burdens the couple will bear. Good premarital counsel likewise names pressures honestly—finances, health, family systems, conflict, and mission—without using difficulty to frighten people away from a good covenant.

Marriage is good without being easy. Honest preparation recognizes both covenant joy and additional responsibility. A church serves couples poorly when it romanticizes marriage or treats singleness as a waiting room.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase refers to ordinary pressures experienced in embodied family life, especially during distress—not to moral impurity in marriage. Paul wants to spare his readers added hardship while plainly affirming that bride and groom do not sin by marrying.

verses.bibleText: But this I say, brethren, the time is short: it remaineth, that both they that have wives be as though they had none;

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“The time is short” can mean compressed or drawn together. Paul lives in the decisive period inaugurated by Christ’s resurrection and moving toward his return. Even when chronology exceeds human expectation, the theological claim remains: the present age is not permanent.

Those with wives are to be “as though” they had none—not by denying intimacy or neglecting covenant, which would contradict verses 3–5, but by refusing to make marriage ultimate. Allegiance to Christ governs marriage rather than disappearing inside it.

Paul does not set a date. “Shortened” describes time after Christ’s decisive act: history has contracted around the coming completion. Every generation lives nearer the return and should refuse to treat delay as cancellation.

Marriage “as though not” means relativization, not neglect. The spouse remains a neighbor to be loved and a covenant to be kept. Christ’s claim prevents the relationship from becoming idolatrous, abusive, or closed to mission.

The verse can actually strengthen marriage by freeing spouses from impossible expectations. Neither must provide the eternal security only Christ can give. The eschatological horizon also forbids indefinite postponement of obedience. Because time is drawn short, believers should repair wrong, use gifts, and order commitments now. “As though” does not create apathy; it creates urgency around what will endure when the present form passes.

Eschatological detachment is not indifference. It permits deeper love because a spouse is received as God’s gift rather than required to bear the weight of being one’s savior, identity, and future.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul views present life from the horizon of Christ’s coming and the brevity of human existence. Marriage remains a real covenant, but it cannot become the believer’s ultimate identity or displace loyalty to the Lord.

verses.bibleText: And they that weep, as though they wept not; and they that rejoice, as though they rejoiced not; and they that buy, as though they possessed not;

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Mourning and rejoicing are both real Christian responses; Paul elsewhere commands believers to share them. “As though” means neither state should be treated as the final definition of reality. God will wipe away tears, and present joy anticipates a greater joy still.

Likewise, buyers should live as people who do not possess. Legal ownership does not become absolute moral ownership. Goods remain entrusted resources within a world passing toward God’s renewal.

Paul’s series covers emotion and economics, the ordinary forces that can fill consciousness. He does not forbid grief, joy, or purchasing. The repeated “as though” gives each a penultimate status.

Christian grief can be deep because loss is real, yet it is not hopeless because resurrection is promised. Christian joy can be exuberant without denying suffering because it does not claim present pleasure is complete.

Possession becomes stewardship. Buyers use legal rights with the knowledge that goods, land, and money remain under God’s claim and should serve love. Buying without possessing challenges the Roman use of property to establish identity and patronal control. Christian ownership remains legally real but morally accountable to the Lord and neighbor. Generosity becomes possible when goods are no longer required to secure an ultimate self.

The verse trains emotional and economic freedom without stoic numbness. Christians grieve honestly, rejoice gratefully, and use possessions responsibly because every present condition is held inside resurrection hope.

wordNotes.explanation: Repeated “as though” language relativizes grief, celebration, and possessions without denying any of them. Each is real, yet none is final. Hope in God prevents sorrow, pleasure, or acquisition from ruling the whole life.

verses.bibleText: And they that use this world, as not abusing it: for the fashion of this world passeth away.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Those who “use this world” must not abuse it or become engrossed in it. The world is God’s creation and the arena of vocation; “the fashion” passing away is its present form under sin, mortality, and distorted power.

“Fashion” translates schēma, the outward form or arrangement. Paul does not say God will discard creation; chapter 15 anticipates transformation and resurrection. What passes is the world configured by mortality and sin.

“Not abusing it” can also be translated “not using it to the full,” avoiding total absorption. The believer participates with restraint because no present institution is ultimate. Such restraint creates room for Sabbath, generosity, care of creation, and service that consumer life crowds out.

This gives public engagement a distinctive posture: neither withdrawal nor captivity. Christians work for justice and care for creation while knowing God’s kingdom, not their success, will complete the work.

This outlook opposes both consumerism and neglect of creation. Temporary stewardship can be careful and generous precisely because the steward does not own the future. God, not the market or empire, determines the world’s final form.

wordNotes.explanation: “Fashion” denotes the world’s present form or outward arrangement. Its structures are passing away, so Christians may use created resources responsibly without exploiting them or fastening their hope to an order that will not last.

verses.bibleText: But I would have you without carefulness. He that is unmarried careth for the things that belong to the Lord, how he may please the Lord:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul wants them “without carefulness”—free from consuming anxiety. He does not promise that single life contains no loneliness, financial concern, family obligation, or distraction. The comparison is relative to the additional responsibilities of marriage in their circumstances.

The unmarried person can give concentrated attention to “the things that belong to the Lord.” That includes prayer and proclamation, but also ordinary work and service offered under Christ.

The term for anxiety can describe either legitimate concern or consuming distraction; verses 33–34 use the same family for marital care. Paul does not promise a psychologically carefree life.

His desired freedom is availability to attend to the Lord. Unmarried believers may possess flexibility in location, schedule, resources, and risk that can serve mission. That freedom is not automatic and may be limited by caregiving, poverty, or health.

Churches should ask how their structures help single people turn availability into belonging and service rather than assume they need no support. Paul’s “I would have you” remains a desire, not a command. His own missionary calling understandably shapes the value he places on mobility and undivided attention. The church receives his wisdom without assuming every vocation demands the same availability.

Singleness becomes a gift when freedom is directed toward love. Without community and purpose, it can be as self-absorbed as any other state. Paul’s vision requires a church family in which unmarried believers truly belong and can invest their lives.

wordNotes.explanation: The older phrase means free from consuming anxiety, not careless or irresponsible. Paul values singleness here because it can reduce divided obligations and create space for concentrated service to the Lord.

verses.bibleText: But he that is married careth for the things that are of the world, how he may please his wife.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
A married man properly cares “how he may please his wife.” Paul does not condemn that concern as worldliness in the sinful sense. Covenant love requires attention to material provision, affection, safety, and a spouse’s good.

The result is divided practical concern: service to the Lord must include responsibilities to the spouse. Paul sees this as a complication under distress, not evidence that the husband is less devoted by definition.

“Things of the world” in this verse means ordinary household affairs, not necessarily sinful worldliness. Pleasing one’s wife is a legitimate marital duty. Paul’s realism prevents ministry from consuming a family under the claim that only church work belongs to the Lord. Providing, listening, sharing labor, and being present to a spouse are forms of Christian faithfulness.

The divided concern is practical, not a divided ultimate loyalty. Marriage becomes disordered when a spouse demands what belongs only to Christ or when religious work is used to evade covenant responsibility.

The husband’s concern can become sinful if pleasing the spouse means disobeying God, but the concern itself is honorable. The verse prevents a false spirituality in which family care is treated as distraction while public ministry is treated as the only service that counts.

Marriage should not be used to excuse spiritual neglect, and ministry should not be used to neglect marriage. The married believer pleases the Lord partly by faithfully caring for the person to whom he has made covenant.

wordNotes.term: Careth … how he may please his wife
wordNotes.explanation: A married man rightly attends to his wife’s wellbeing. Paul names this responsibility as a practical division of attention, not a moral failure; covenant care becomes a problem only when it displaces obedience to the Lord.

verses.bibleText: There is difference also between a wife and a virgin. The unmarried woman careth for the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit: but she that is married careth for the things of the world, how she may please her husband.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul applies the same analysis to unmarried and married women. The textual punctuation is difficult: “there is a difference also between a wife and a virgin” may end verse 33 or begin verse 34. Either way, the contrast concerns differing responsibilities.

The unmarried woman may be “holy both in body and in spirit,” dedicating her whole life to the Lord. This does not imply that a married woman is bodily unholy. Marital sexuality has already been affirmed.

Paul’s concern for bodily holiness in the unmarried woman should be read beside 6:13–20. Celibacy is embodied discipleship, not escape from the body. Desire is governed and the body made available to service.

“Spirit” refers to the inward life oriented to God. Body and spirit together express comprehensive dedication without suggesting detachable substances. For the married woman, concern to please her husband likewise belongs within discipleship. Paul describes differing distributions of attention, not two levels of holiness.

“Holy in body” may particularly answer the chapter’s opening ascetic question. An unmarried woman can dedicate embodied life without despising it; a married woman serves God through embodied covenant. Holiness is not absence of sex but bodily life belonging to the Lord in the state given.

In a society that often defined a woman through husband, household, and childbearing, Paul’s recognition of an unmarried woman’s direct vocation to the Lord is socially significant. Her calling requires neither male ownership nor apology.

wordNotes.term: Holy both in body and in spirit
wordNotes.explanation: Holiness embraces the whole person rather than a supposedly spiritual part detached from the body. The unmarried woman may devote her embodied life more directly to God, though Paul does not imply that a faithful wife is unholy.

verses.bibleText: And this I speak for your own profit; not that I may cast a snare upon you, but for that which is comely, and that ye may attend upon the Lord without distraction.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul states his motive: “for your own profit,” not to cast a “snare” or noose around them. His preference for singleness is counsel intended to promote honorable and undistracted devotion, not a new law by which he can control private decisions.

“Attend upon the Lord without distraction” describes settled, available service. The same principle can guide either state: marriage or singleness should be chosen and lived in a way that permits truthful devotion.

The word translated “comely” can mean honorable, fitting, or seemly. Paul seeks a stable life that corresponds to devotion rather than a socially impressive appearance.

“Without distraction” is a strong term for undivided attendance. It should not be turned into a claim that every single person must accept unlimited church work. Service remains bounded by creaturely health, rest, and other callings.

The noose image is especially relevant wherever religious leaders control courtship or require celibacy. Paul explicitly disowns counsel that traps. A noose restricts movement and can become an instrument of death. Paul chooses a forceful denial because even sound counsel about singleness can become oppressive when institutionalized. The apostle will not secure devotion through trapped conscience. Worship worthy of Christ is free, truthful, and sustained.

The verse gives a test for pastoral counsel. Advice becomes un-Pauline when it produces manipulation, shame, or dependence on the counselor. Sound guidance offers reasons, respects gift and consent, and seeks the person’s good before the Lord.

wordNotes.term: Attend upon the Lord without distraction
wordNotes.explanation: Paul’s aim is steady, undivided service rather than control. He expressly refuses to “cast a snare,” so his counsel about singleness must not become pressure that binds consciences God has left free.

verses.bibleText: But if any man think that he behaveth himself uncomely toward his virgin, if she pass the flower of her age, and need so require, let him do what he will, he sinneth not: let them marry.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The Greek and social setting make this verse difficult. The man may be a fiancé concerned that he is acting improperly toward the woman to whom he is betrothed; another reading sees a father or guardian deciding about a daughter. The first fits the language of desire and “let them marry,” while the second reflects ancient household authority. Certainty is not possible.

The phrase translated “pass the flower of her age” can refer to the woman being beyond marriageable age or to passion becoming strong; this contributes to the interpretive uncertainty. “So require” signals a genuine necessity perceived in the relationship.

Paul trusts the decision enough to say, “let him do what he will,” but freedom must be read under mutual covenant and “let them marry.” The action concerns both people.

No family honor, property plan, or spiritual ambition can make coerced marriage Christian. Consent, capacity, and public covenant are essential to the honorable course Paul permits.

On either reading, marriage is permitted: “he sinneth not.” If desire, age, obligation, and circumstances make marriage the truthful course, ascetic reputation should not prevent it.

“Let them marry” is plural and shifts attention from the man’s judgment to the couple’s union. Whatever asymmetry the ancient setting assumes, the outcome is a shared covenant. Contemporary application should make that plural reality explicit through mutual consent and preparation.

Ancient patriarchal assumptions cannot be imported as timeless permission to decide a woman’s future without her consent. Christian marriage requires the willing covenant of those being joined. The verse resolves conscience; it does not authorize coercion.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase may describe a man’s betrothed woman or, less likely, a daughter under a father’s care; the precise situation is debated. Paul’s clear conclusion is that marriage under the stated conditions is permitted and is not sin.

verses.bibleText: Nevertheless he that standeth stedfast in his heart, having no necessity, but hath power over his own will, and hath so decreed in his heart that he will keep his virgin, doeth well.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The alternative is a settled decision not to marry. Paul describes firmness of heart, absence of compulsion, authority over one’s will, and deliberate judgment. Singleness is commended where freedom and capacity converge, not where another person imposes it.

“Having no necessity” may refer to external pressure or inward sexual need. The person is not driven helplessly in either direction. Self-control permits a real choice.

The language of standing “stedfast” contrasts with being driven by necessity. A settled decision can mature over time; firmness should not be confused with rigidity that refuses new truth or changed circumstances.

“Power over his own will” means self-government, not power over the woman. Even on a guardian reading, the decision must be interpreted through Paul’s earlier mutuality and the woman’s status before the Lord.

Celibacy is commendable when chosen, supported, and oriented to service. Imposed celibacy can produce fear and secrecy rather than the undistracted devotion Paul values.

Self-command in this verse resembles Paul’s later athletic discipline. It is not denial of creaturely desire but ordered agency under a larger purpose. The decision remains good only while it can be held without secret exploitation, manipulation of another, or contempt for marriage.

If a betrothed couple is in view, the woman’s settled calling and freedom must be included even though Paul speaks from the man’s perspective. One party cannot preserve personal celibacy by binding another to indefinite uncertainty. Honest release may be necessary.

wordNotes.explanation: The decision described is free from compulsion and made with settled conviction. Paul values deliberate self-government, yet the verse belongs to a difficult case and should not be detached from the equal goodness of marriage affirmed around it.

verses.bibleText: So then he that giveth her in marriage doeth well; but he that giveth her not in marriage doeth better.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s summary gives two positive verdicts: the one who marries “doeth well,” and the one who does not “doeth better.” “Better” is comparative counsel within the present distress and Paul’s concern for undistracted service; it is not a hierarchy of human worth or saving holiness.

The verse also keeps desire from becoming destiny. A couple may marry faithfully, or people genuinely gifted for singleness may refrain. Both actions can honor the Lord.

“Doeth better” should be allowed its real comparative force without becoming universal law. Under Paul’s reasons, singleness can offer a strategic advantage. In another situation, marriage may better serve faithfulness, protection, partnership, and mission.

The evaluation concerns action, not intrinsic worth. Neither wedding nor celibacy changes one’s standing in Christ. Paul states a preference, gives reasons, protects marriage from the charge of sin, and leaves responsible choice; mature pastoral counsel can do the same.

Paul’s comparative counsel is also temporally situated: the present distress and passing form of the world have occupied the paragraph. The church errs if it lifts “better” out of those reasons and creates a permanent caste in which celibate leaders are inherently holier.

Churches should preserve Paul’s double affirmation. Marriage must not be demeaned as spiritual compromise, and singleness must not be treated as deficient adulthood. Wisdom asks which state can be received freely and lived faithfully in the actual circumstances.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul compares two faithful choices under the chapter’s present circumstances. “Better” concerns practical freedom for devotion amid distress, not greater purity or spiritual worth; the one who marries still “doeth well.”

verses.bibleText: The wife is bound by the law as long as her husband liveth; but if her husband be dead, she is at liberty to be married to whom she will; only in the Lord.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
A wife is bound to her husband while he lives; death brings the earthly marriage covenant to its close. Paul affirms permanence while addressing a widow’s future with remarkable freedom: she may marry “whom she will.” Her consent and judgment matter.

“Only in the Lord” directs a believer to marry a fellow believer. Shared allegiance to Christ affects worship, conscience, family practice, stewardship, and hope; it is not a decorative preference after other compatibilities are assessed.

“Bound by the law” in the KJV expresses covenant obligation; some early manuscripts omit “by the law,” but all retain that the wife is bound while the husband lives. Death, not personal dissatisfaction, ends the bond in this case.

“Whom she will” was meaningful in a culture where family and property interests could dominate a widow’s remarriage. Christian community should protect, not override, that agency.

“In the Lord” concerns more than nominal identity. A prospective spouse’s actual allegiance, character, and willingness to share discipleship matter. The phrase does not guarantee a painless marriage; it establishes the indispensable common Lord.

A widow’s freedom also implies protection from levirate-style or family pressure wrongly transferred into a setting where the law does not require it. “Whom she will” honors discernment; “in the Lord” gives discernment a covenant boundary. Freedom and obedience appear in the same sentence.

The verse specifically concerns widowhood. It should not be made to settle every disputed remarriage case without the teaching of Jesus, verses 10–15, and pastoral attention to adultery, desertion, repentance, and safety. It clearly says that remarriage after a spouse’s death is honorable and free from guilt.

wordNotes.explanation: A widow is free to remarry, but her freedom remains under Christ’s lordship. The phrase ordinarily directs a believer toward a marriage shared in Christian faith, preserving spiritual loyalty within the new covenant.

verses.bibleText: But she is happier if she so abide, after my judgment: and I think also that I have the Spirit of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul judges that the widow may be “happier,” or more blessed, if she remains unmarried. The reasons given earlier still govern: distress, shortened time, and freedom for service. He does not command the decision and has just affirmed her liberty to marry.

“I think also that I have the Spirit of God” does not express uncertainty about inspiration. It may carry gentle irony toward Corinthians who claimed spiritual insight for their ascetic views: Paul modestly but firmly asserts that his pastoral judgment is Spirit-guided.

The adjective translated “happier” can mean blessed. Paul makes a judgment about flourishing under the conditions he has described, not an absolute promise that unmarried widowhood will feel emotionally easier.

The final Spirit reference also recalls the chapter’s central balance. Guidance honors Jesus’ command, apostolic wisdom, bodily reality, mutual consent, covenant permanence, and freedom for service. Spirituality that ignores those concrete features is not the spirituality Paul claims.

Paul’s preference should not be used to shame a widow who remarries. He has explicitly declared her free. A blessing is not a command, and another believer’s different gift does not nullify it. The Spirit’s guidance can lead people into differing faithful futures without contradiction.

The chapter ends as it began—with goodness rather than compulsion. Marriage is good; singleness can be blessed; neither condition saves. The Spirit leads believers to receive a calling truthfully, honor covenant, protect peace, and serve the Lord in the state they freely and faithfully inhabit.

wordNotes.term: I think also that I have the Spirit of God
wordNotes.explanation: Paul’s restrained wording answers those who might dismiss his judgment as merely private preference. He does not boast of superiority; he quietly claims that his pastoral discernment is formed by the Spirit given to Christ’s apostle.
